text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY PROGRAM
TUMOR BOARD CONSULTATION NOTE
Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology / Head and Neck Surgery

PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Referring Physician: Otolaryngology (ENT)
Attending Physicians: Head and Neck Medical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Head and Neck Surgery
Note Type: New Patient Multidisciplinary Consultation / Tumor Board Presentation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American woman, never-smoker, who presents for multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment planning of a newly diagnosed HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, T2N1M0, Stage I per AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx classification).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo presented to her primary care physician approximately 11 weeks ago with a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort and globus sensation. She described mild odynophagia and referred left-sided otalgia, which was particularly notable during swallowing. On that initial PCP visit, a firm, non-tender 2.5 cm lymph node was palpated in the left level II cervical chain. She was empirically treated with a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for presumed pharyngitis. She returned approximately 5 weeks later with persistent symptoms and no reduction in the cervical lymphadenopathy, prompting referral to Otolaryngology.

Otolaryngology performed a thorough evaluation including flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy, which demonstrated a suspicious left base of tongue mass. A CT scan of the neck with contrast was obtained, showing a 2.2 cm enhancing mass at the left base of tongue with extension toward the left vallecula, and multiple left level II/IIA lymph nodes with the dominant node measuring 2.7 cm and demonstrating central necrosis — highly concerning for metastatic adenopathy. Directed biopsy of the left base of tongue mass was performed in clinic under local anesthesia. Pathology returned squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, with strong diffuse p16 positivity in a block-positive pattern (>70% of cells) and high-risk HPV RNA positivity by in situ hybridization. Keratinizing morphology was absent, consistent with HPV-driven pathogenesis.

For complete staging, the patient underwent a PET-CT scan, which confirmed an intensely FDG-avid left base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2) and a hypermetabolic left level IIA lymph node measuring 2.6 cm (SUVmax 11.8) with a central photopenic area consistent with necrosis. No contralateral cervical involvement, no retropharyngeal nodal disease, and no evidence of distant metastatic disease were identified. No synchronous primary was found. Fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA node under ultrasound guidance confirmed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma with p16-positive staining on cell block. Comprehensive next-generation sequencing (500-gene panel) was performed on the primary biopsy specimen and is notable for a PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation (VAF 18%), TRAF3 p.R118* loss-of-function mutation (VAF 22%), EP300 p.R418C mutation (VAF 15%), FBXW7 p.R505C (VAF 12%), and KMT2D truncating mutation (VAF 19%). Tumor mutational burden is low at 4 mutations/Mb, microsatellite stable, PD-L1 CPS 18, and no TP53 mutation — the latter consistent with HPV-mediated functional p53 suppression via E6 protein. HPV integration was detected at chromosome 8q24. No actionable fusions or EGFR/FGFR amplifications identified.

Formal AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx) staging: T2N1M0, Stage I. The patient was referred to the multidisciplinary head and neck oncology program for definitive treatment planning and is presenting today for tumor board discussion and treatment recommendation.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Hypothyroidism — well-controlled, on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; most recent TSH within normal limits.
2. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) — mild, managed with dietary modifications and occasional antacids; no PPI currently prescribed.
3. No prior malignancies.
4. No prior head and neck surgery or radiation.
5. No diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.
6. Baseline swallowing evaluation by speech-language pathology (performed in preparation for this consultation) demonstrated mild-to-moderate oropharyngeal dysphagia with penetration on thin liquids; no frank aspiration documented.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

1. Appendectomy in early adulthood (remote, uncomplicated).
2. No prior head and neck procedures beyond the recent diagnostic laryngoscopy and biopsy.

FAMILY HISTORY

No family history of head and neck cancers, oropharyngeal malignancy, or HPV-related cancers. Mother: alive, hypertension. Father: deceased from myocardial infarction in his late 70s. No first-degree relatives with malignancy.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Okonkwo is a high school English teacher and finds her work meaningful. She is married and has two adult children who are supportive. She is a lifelong never-smoker (0 pack-years). She denies any alcohol use. She denies recreational drug use. She is of Nigerian-American descent. She lives approximately 25 minutes from our cancer center and has reliable transportation. She is college-educated and highly engaged in her medical care, arriving today with written questions and a list of concerns. She has strong social support. She is pre-menopausal by history.

ALLERGIES

NKDA (No known drug allergies). No documented contrast allergies; patient tolerated IV contrast without incident during recent CT and PET-CT.

MEDICATIONS

1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (hypothyroidism)
2. Calcium carbonate/antacid PRN (GERD)
3. No anticoagulants, immunosuppressants, or other oncologically relevant medications.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Persistent left-sided throat discomfort (improving somewhat from nadir), mild odynophagia, left otalgia (referred), globus sensation, mild difficulty swallowing thin liquids (pre-existing per SLP evaluation). Moderate anxiety related to diagnosis.
Negative: No hemoptysis, no hoarseness or voice changes, no stridor or airway compromise, no trismus, no otalgia at rest (only with swallowing), no significant weight loss prior to diagnosis (weight is stable at 68 kg, BMI 24.1), no night sweats, no B symptoms, no dyspnea, no chest pain, no new skin lesions, no neurologic symptoms. No significant dysphagia to solids at baseline (prior to diagnosis).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Well-nourished, well-hydrated. Engaged and appropriate affect.
Vital Signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, Temperature 98.4°F, Weight 68 kg, Height 167 cm, BMI 24.1.
ECOG Performance Status: 0.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat:
- Head: normocephalic, atraumatic
- Eyes: no scleral icterus, pupils equal and reactive
- Ears: pinnae normal bilaterally; external auditory canals clear; tympanic membranes intact with normal landmarks bilaterally — no evidence of middle ear effusion or serous otitis media
- Nose: no nasal obstruction, no epistaxis
- Oral cavity: mucosa intact, moist; teeth in good condition; no mucosal lesions; dentition preserved (dental prophylaxis recently completed); no trismus (inter-incisal distance >35 mm)
- Oropharynx: flexible laryngoscopy performed at this visit confirms a visible mucosal irregularity and submucosal fullness of the left base of tongue; no ulceration visible on today's examination; right base of tongue, bilateral tonsillar fossae, posterior pharyngeal wall without suspicious lesions; epiglottis and supraglottis normal; vocal cords fully mobile and symmetric; no subglottic or tracheal lesion

Neck:
- Left neck: A firm, moderately mobile, non-tender lymph node palpable in the left level IIA position, estimated 2.5 cm on clinical examination, consistent with known metastatic adenopathy. No tenderness to palpation. No overlying skin changes.
- Right neck: No palpable lymphadenopathy
- No palpable thyroid nodule or goiter
- No carotid bruits
- Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius intact

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or rhonchi.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly.
Extremities: No peripheral edema, no clubbing or cyanosis.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII intact; tongue protrusion midline; no facial asymmetry; shoulder shrug symmetric; no peripheral motor or sensory deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count: WBC 6.2 x10^9/L (normal differential), Hgb 13.8 g/dL, Hct 41.2%, Platelets 247 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2, ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Total Bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Albumin 4.0 g/dL, Alkaline Phosphatase 68 U/L.
TSH: 1.6 mIU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine 75 mcg).
Audiologic Baseline: Pure tone audiometry performed (baseline for cisplatin monitoring) — within normal limits bilaterally.
Dental clearance: Completed. All necessary extractions and prophylaxis performed. Fluoride tray fabrication initiated.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Neck with Contrast (recent): 2.2 cm enhancing mass at the left base of tongue with extension toward the left vallecula. Multiple left level II/IIA lymph nodes, largest 2.7 cm with central necrosis, consistent with metastatic adenopathy. No contralateral lymphadenopathy. Chest windows without evidence of distant disease.

PET-CT (staging): Intensely FDG-avid left base of tongue primary, SUVmax 14.2. Hypermetabolic left level IIA node (SUVmax 11.8) with central photopenic area (necrosis). No contralateral cervical, retropharyngeal, or distant FDG-avid disease. No synchronous primary. Staging: T2N1M0, Stage I (AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive oropharynx).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Impression: Ms. Okonkwo is a 52-year-old never-smoking woman with newly diagnosed HPV-positive (p16+, HPV RNA ISH+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, AJCC 8th edition T2N1M0, Stage I (HPV-positive oropharynx classification), with a single ipsilateral necrotic level IIA metastatic lymph node. Her overall health is excellent (ECOG PS 0, normal organ function, BMI 24.1, no significant comorbidities). This is a locoregional disease with highly favorable HPV-positive biology.

Case was formally presented and discussed at today's Multidisciplinary Head and Neck Tumor Board. Attendees included Head and Neck Medical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Head and Neck Surgery, Diagnostic Radiology, Pathology, Speech-Language Pathology, Nutrition/Dietetics, Dental Oncology, and Oncology Social Work.

1. TREATMENT RECOMMENDATION — DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION:

After full multidisciplinary discussion, the consensus recommendation is definitive concurrent chemoradiation with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) and cisplatin-based chemotherapy. This is the standard-of-care approach for this disease, supported by robust level 1 evidence from multiple phase III trials (including RTOG 0129, and the MACH-NC meta-analysis). The expected locoregional control and overall survival rates for HPV-positive stage I (T2N1) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma with concurrent chemoradiation are excellent, with 5-year disease-specific survival rates exceeding 85-90% in comparable cohorts.

Radiation: IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique using volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT) with daily CBCT image guidance. The radiation oncology team has outlined a plan of 70 Gy to the high-risk PTV in 35 fractions, with elective nodal volumes at intermediate and low-risk doses. Detailed dosimetry and organ-at-risk constraints (parotid glands, spinal cord, brainstem, mandible, larynx) will be finalized at simulation.

Cisplatin: High-dose bolus cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 22, and 43 of radiation (3 planned cycles), per RTOG/NRG standard. Pre-hydration with 3 L normal saline per cycle, with standard antiemetics (ondansetron, dexamethasone) and magnesium supplementation. Baseline renal function is adequate (creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, GFR >60 mL/min). Audiologic baseline is documented.

Rationale for standard-dose cisplatin (vs. weekly carboplatin or cetuximab): Given the patient's excellent performance status, normal renal function, baseline audiogram, and never-smoker status with HPV-positive disease, the established high-dose cisplatin regimen was favored for its superior locoregional control data. Substitution with cetuximab would be reserved for patients with significant cisplatin contraindications.

2. DE-ESCALATION CLINICAL TRIAL CONSIDERATION:

The tumor board discussed at length the patient's eligibility for available institutional and cooperative group de-escalation trials for HPV-positive low-risk oropharyngeal cancer (e.g., reduced-dose radiation or reduced-intensity chemotherapy). The favorable disease characteristics — HPV positivity, never-smoker, T2 primary, single ipsilateral node, complete response to be expected — make her a biologically appropriate candidate for de-escalation principles as explored in NRG-HN002, ORATOR2, and related trials. However, after careful review, the multidisciplinary team determined that the patient is ineligible for the current institutional de-escalation trial due to the presence of central nodal necrosis in the involved left level IIA lymph node, which is an explicit exclusion criterion on the open protocol. She will be treated with standard-of-care concurrent chemoradiation.

3. PEG TUBE AND NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT:

The speech-language pathology team presented the results of the baseline modified swallowing evaluation, which showed mild-to-moderate oropharyngeal dysphagia with penetration on thin liquids at baseline. Given the anticipated severity and duration of radiation-induced mucositis (70 Gy to the oropharynx with concurrent cisplatin), the nutrition team noted high risk for treatment-related weight loss and prolonged inability to maintain adequate oral intake. Prophylactic PEG tube placement was discussed in detail with the patient. The decision was made that the patient would undergo PEG tube placement prior to the initiation of chemoradiation, as a multidisciplinary decision, both for nutritional optimization during treatment and for safety given her pre-existing dysphagia. The patient was fully counseled on PEG tube care and enteral feeding protocols. Pre-treatment weight optimization via supplemental enteral nutrition will begin promptly.

4. DENTAL CLEARANCE AND RADIATION PLANNING:

Dental clearance has been completed with all necessary extractions and prophylaxis performed. Fluoride trays have been initiated. Radiation simulation CT will be scheduled imminently for treatment planning purposes.

5. SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT AND SUPPORTIVE CARE:

The patient will be closely monitored throughout treatment for mucositis (anticipated to be grade 2-3), radiation dermatitis, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, and fatigue. She will be seen weekly during radiation by both medical oncology and radiation oncology. A dedicated oncology dietitian will monitor weight and enteral feeding throughout. Oncology social work met with the patient today to establish support resources, including connecting her with the HPV-positive head and neck cancer support group and online patient resources.

6. MONITORING PARAMETERS:

- Weekly CBCs and basic metabolic panels during chemoradiation, with additional creatinine/electrolytes checked prior to each cisplatin dose
- Audiology re-check at cycle 3 (day 43)
- Serum magnesium monitoring given cisplatin-associated wasting
- Levothyroxine and thyroid function: TSH to be rechecked mid-treatment and post-treatment given risk of radiation-induced hypothyroidism
- Post-treatment response assessment PET-CT to be scheduled at 10-12 weeks post-completion of radiation to allow adequate resolution of treatment-related inflammatory changes

7. DISPOSITION:

The patient was seen today for her multidisciplinary consultation. She understands the diagnosis, staging, proposed treatment plan, anticipated toxicities, and rationale for standard-of-care versus de-escalation approaches. She expressed clear understanding and wishes to proceed with the recommended treatment. Informed consent for concurrent chemoradiation will be obtained separately by radiation oncology and medical oncology. PEG tube placement will be coordinated with interventional gastroenterology this week. Radiation simulation will be scheduled as soon as PEG placement is completed and the patient has healed.

Patient voiced appreciation for the thorough multidisciplinary process and expressed particular concern about maintaining her ability to speak and teach post-treatment. She was reassured that HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma treated with this regimen carries an excellent prognosis and that the speech-language pathology team will work with her throughout and after treatment to optimize functional recovery.

Follow-up: Treatment to begin within approximately 2 weeks. Patient will be seen by radiation oncology for simulation and by medical oncology for pre-chemoradiation counseling and cycle 1 planning visit before first treatment day.

Electronically signed:
Multidisciplinary Head and Neck Oncology Program
Head and Neck Medical Oncology
Radiation Oncology
Head and Neck Surgery
[Tumor Board Attending Physicians — see separate attestation]
